meta.title: nihil

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: nihil
root.mentor_note: nihil is a stark little root, and it only ever built a handful of words, each one just as stark.

attempt.direction_prompt: A gentle decline, or destruction, reduced to nothing?

context_sentences: A single flood had annihilated three seasons of careful planting in one night.
The champion annihilated every challenger that year, never once losing a set.
The novel’s narrator flirts with nihilism for two hundred pages before quietly abandoning it.
His nihilism, friends noticed, always seemed to vanish whenever something actually mattered to him.
The match ended two nil, a scoreline nobody in the stadium had predicted at half time.
Support for the proposal was practically nil once the true cost became public.

construct_options.text: Nothing at all; zero, especially as a score or amount
A belief that life has no inherent meaning or value
A small but steadily growing amount

context_sentences: The film’s nihilistic ending left half the audience arguing on the way out of the theatre.
His nihilistic phase in his twenties gave way, later, to a surprisingly settled sense of purpose.

construct_options.text: Rejecting moral or religious value, as though nothing ultimately matters
Completely destroyed, reduced entirely to nothing
Deeply devoted to a religious or moral cause
